<email>
From: alicia.morrison@email.com
To: support@fabricsafe.com
Subject: FabricSafe we need to talk about the flagging
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T16:30:00
Body: FabricSafe team,

I need to be direct — I'm seriously considering switching to a different app. Over the past month, you've flagged or raised safety concerns about almost every fabric I've submitted for review. I've been making purchasing decisions based on your assessments and it's cost me real money and put my nursery project way behind schedule.

Out of the last 7 fabrics I submitted, you flagged 5 of them — including fabrics with GOTS and Oeko-Tex certifications. I returned two bolts of fabric based on your flags, which cost me $120 in returns and expedited shipping on replacements. Then I brought your specific concerns to my daughter's pediatric dermatologist. She reviewed each one and said she would have had no issue with any of the fabrics I returned. She said they were all appropriate choices for a baby with eczema, and that I was "overthinking this based on overly cautious app recommendations."

My daughter's nursery still doesn't have proper crib bedding because every time I find a fabric, your app gives me a reason to second-guess it. Meanwhile, she's sleeping on a bare waterproof mattress cover because I'm too anxious to commit to any fabric. That's not a good outcome for anyone, least of all my baby.

I understand that eczema adds complexity and you're trying to be careful. But when you flag GOTS-certified organic cotton alongside fabrics that have genuinely concerning chemical treatments, I can't tell which flags actually matter. Everything gets the same level of concern, which means nothing gets useful differentiation.

Here's where I am: if the next set of reviews comes back with the same pattern, I'm going to cancel my account and just follow my pediatric dermatologist's recommendations directly. Or I'll switch to the app my friend Megan uses — she says it gives her clear, practical fabric guidance that distinguishes real concerns from theoretical ones.

I need this nursery finished. I need fabrics on that crib. Help me make good decisions instead of making me afraid of every option.

Alicia
</email>

<email>
From: alicia.morrison@email.com
To: support@fabricsafe.com
Subject: Talked to my pediatric dermatologist about this
Timestamp: 2025-10-10T17:45:11
Body: Hi,

I had a longer conversation with my daughter's pediatric dermatologist about the fabric flagging situation. She was very clear.

She said that "moderate eczema" in a 10-month-old is common, manageable, and does not mean the baby can only touch laboratory-grade sterile materials. She said the compromised skin barrier concern is real but context-dependent — it matters most for chemical irritants that are actively present on the fabric surface, not for trace residuals that are below certified safety thresholds. She said GOTS and Oeko-Tex Class 1 certifications are designed to account for sensitive infant skin and that she trusts those certifications in her clinical practice.

She also said something that really stuck with me: the biggest fabric-related risk for an eczema baby is actually using the wrong detergent or not pre-washing fabrics enough. The fabric material itself — assuming it's a natural fiber from a certified source — is rarely the problem. She said she'd rather see a baby on pre-washed GOTS organic cotton than on a bare mattress cover because a safety app flagged every fabric option.

My friend Megan made the same point from a practical angle. She said when a fabric safety app flags everything with the same level of concern, parents either give up on the project entirely or start ignoring the flags — and then they might miss a flag about a fabric that actually has a concerning chemical treatment. Both outcomes are worse than practical, calibrated guidance.

I want to keep using FabricSafe. I genuinely need a tool for this project. But the reviews need to be calibrated to real pediatric practice — flag fabrics that a dermatologist would actually tell me to avoid, and approve the ones that are safe. Use the full range of ratings. Not everything needs a warning.

- Alicia
</email>
